column3: Documentation, migration notes, historical references, and accepted coexistence policy.
Top-level project documentation may still refer to the upstream repo name.
Build and release automation may still contain legacy OpenClash labels, upstream repo coordinates, or package-branch metadata references.
Issue templates still target the existing upstream-facing repository workflows.
Repository guidance still documents upstream context.
Validation and helper tooling may intentionally mention legacy names while cleanup is ongoing.
Package metadata and lifecycle surface may still contain upstream repository naming.
Translations are inherited content and may still contain OpenClash strings until localization cleanup is done.
Live init/runtime ownership path.
Install-time defaults and shared-system mutation path.
Default config may intentionally reference upstream source-policy values such as core_source=openclash and upstream overwrite URLs.
Clash Nivo-owned persistent files that still carry inherited custom file names or comments.
Intentional coexistence detection and status reporting for a separately installed OpenClash instance.
Frontend service-status typing intentionally exposes OpenClash coexistence fields returned by the backend guard/status model.
Service update layer may intentionally branch on upstream source-policy values such as core_source=openclash.
Live service modules and runtime control surfaces not covered by narrower intentional categories.
Inherited runtime helper internals still using legacy names.
Update/version helpers may intentionally reference upstream package/core source URLs and repository coordinates.
Core source helper intentionally models upstream source-policy modes and repository coordinates.
Inherited library/helper internals still using legacy names.
Import area may intentionally reference OpenClash as a migration source.
Top-level compatibility wrapper scripts and entrypoints.
Neutral wrapper entrypoints still bridge into inherited openclash_* helper implementations during staged cleanup.
Neutral wrapper entrypoint remains while the helper-tree cleanup is staged, but it now points at Clash Nivo-owned network discovery logic.
Shared helper entrypoints in the inherited helper tree.
Resource lists may intentionally point at upstream or OpenClash-compatible ecosystems during transition.
Frontend code may still point at inherited Clash Nivo custom-file names during transition.
UI page copy may intentionally describe OpenClash guard and import behavior.
Mock data may still simulate inherited OpenClash paths and guard states.
UI and integration tests may intentionally exercise OpenClash coexistence, migration, and legacy-path behavior.
